channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Plans to Boost Weapons Production Might Not Deliver for Years
item.description: While the defense industry has announced plans to make more munitions, much of that expanded production will not quickly kick in.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The administration does not yet have the funding or congressional support for the weapons it needs for the possible future wars.

item.title: Hegseth Says Iran Cease-Fire Stops Clock for Congressional Approval
item.description: The defense secretary testified on the eve of the 60-day mark of the war, a major statutory deadline for the president to withdraw forces or seek approval from Congress to continue the fight.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified for the second straight day on Capitol Hill on Thursday.

item.title: Takeaways From Hegseth’s Second Day of Testimony on the Iran War
item.description: The secretary was also questioned over civilian deaths, an accusation of antisemitic remarks and women in combat.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, left, and Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, testifying before the Senate Armed Services Committee on Thursday.

item.title: Oil Companies’ Huge Profits Revive Calls for Temporary Windfall Taxes
item.description: European nations imposed temporary taxes in the 2022 energy shock when Russia invaded Ukraine, but whether they can effectively help households is up for debate.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Exxon Mobil refinery in Texas. The United States has not been hit as hard by energy price increases as countries in Asia and Europe.

item.title: Supreme Court Voting Rights Ruling Could Fuel New Era of Redistricting Wars
item.description: The expected flood of new congressional maps is likely to produce fewer competitive districts, fewer ways for voters to hold elected officials accountable and more polarized politics.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling on the Voting Rights Act could unleash another redistricting arms race.

item.title: How Conservatives on the Supreme Court Weakened the Voting Rights Act
item.description: The Supreme Court just overturned Louisiana’s congressional voting map, landing the latest blow to the landmark Voting Rights Act. Abbie VanSickle, a reporter covering the court for The New York Times, explains.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle, Nikolay Nikolov, Paul Abowd, Rafaela Balster and Whitney Shefte

item.title: What Peak Gerrymandering Could Look Like Now
item.description: The redrawing of America’s congressional districts is sure to escalate after the Supreme Court’s decision, with some maps that would have seemed laughable a year ago.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Here’s What States Might Do After the Voting Rights Decision
item.description: The effect of the Supreme Court’s ruling could be as little as one House seat in Louisiana in 2026, but pressure is building on Tennessee and South Carolina Republicans to act.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane, Eduardo Medina and Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: A first-time voter casting their ballot in Atlanta in 2024. Georgia is unlikely to change its maps, but other states are considering it more strongly.

item.title: FEMA Is Reversing Job Cuts Made Under Kristi Noem
item.description: The agency said staff members who had been let go or placed on administrative leave were now needed to prepare for hurricanes and the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Al Drago/Getty Images
item.media:description: Former Homeland Security Kristi Noem during a visit to FEMA headquarters in Washington in January.

item.title: Comey Indictment Shows Justice Dept. Got the Message From Bondi’s Firing
item.description: In naming only an interim successor as acting attorney general, President Trump has established even greater incentives to execute his most extreme demands, current and former officials say.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, who is seeking to become the permanent attorney general, is currently examining a range of other potential charges against James B. Comey.

item.title: House Passes DHS Funding Bill, Ending Shutdown
item.description: Republicans were forced to use a special maneuver to steer around opposition in their own party and speed the measure to the floor, relying on Democratic cooperation to push it through.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The legislation pays for Department of Homeland Security operations through Sept. 30, except for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and parts of border patrol, which received an earlier influx of money from a Republican-only bill.

item.title: Decoding the King: Brits Hear Subtle Rebuke to Trump that Americans Might Miss
item.description: Royal watchers in Britain called the visit of King Charles III to America a master class in understated criticism.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with King Charles III at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: Last on King Charles’s U.S. Tour: A Potluck and a Win for Scottish Whisky
item.description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla had a ceremonial farewell at the White House before attending a block party in Front Royal, Va.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The president and the first lady bidding farewell to King Charles III and Queen Camilla on Thursday.

item.title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ Review: A Rhapsody in Cerulean
item.description: In this sequel, Andy (Anne Hathaway) and Miranda (Meryl Streep) encounter a series of crises that set the stage for a larger, existential catastrophe.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Paraguay Says It’s Not Abandoning Taiwan. China Has Other Plans.
item.description: Beijing is trying to unravel an unlikely long-distance relationship that has endured for decades. Could Paraguay be tempted to stray?
item.dc:creator: Laurence Blair and María Magdalena Arréllaga

item.title: Why Countries Are Stocking Up on Gold
item.description: As risk has escalated, central banks have bought more gold to stash in reserve. A widening Middle East war could add to the urgency.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

item.title: Where in the World Is All That Gold Stored?
item.description: As central banks buy more gold, where to put all that heavy metal is an increasingly important question. Reserves must be secure and ready to trade in a crisis.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: More than 60 central banks store gold at the Bank of England.

item.title: Even Mainers Who Love Janet Mills Are Relieved to See Her Leave Race
item.description: Voters who watched Gov. Janet Mills struggle to gain traction in the Democratic Senate primary said they were unsurprised — and in many cases, ready — to see her exit.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell and Kathleen Tomaselli
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters who had watched Gov. Janet Mills, right, a Democrat, struggle to gain traction in the race said they were largely unsurprised.

item.title: Schumer and Platner Talk After Mills Suspends Her Campaign
item.description: The top Senate Democrat and the Maine contender had what was described as a cordial conversation despite Schumer’s backing of his primary opponent.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate in Maine’s Senate race, speaking at a town hall in Sabattus, Maine, on Wednesday.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Possible Suicide Note Hidden From Public View
item.description: An inmate said he discovered the note after Mr. Epstein was found injured in his jail cell, weeks before his death. It’s now locked in a courthouse.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser, Steve Eder and Jan Ransom

item.title: Epstein Victims’ Pursuit of Justice Finds a New Venue: Albany, N.Y.
item.description: Two victims of Jeffrey Epstein will testify at the State Capitol next week in support of a bill that would enable them to seek punitive damages from his estate.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times

item.title: Sheinbaum’s Dilemma in Mexico: Defy the U.S. or Arrest an Ally
item.description: The Trump administration wants President Claudia Sheinbaum to arrest a Mexican governor. She is faced with few good options in response.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Yuri Cortez/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico could arrest one of her party allies, the governor of Sinaloa state, and extradite him to the United States, or defy the U.S. request and protect him.

item.title: Chonkers the ‘Food-Motivated’ Sea Lion Plops Into San Francisco
item.description: Wildlife experts have been tracking the Steller sea lion since he appeared last month at a popular tourist spot near the end of Fisherman’s Wharf.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Monika Cvorak/The New York Times

item.title: India Is Prototyping a Dangerous New Model of Web Censorship
item.description: The Modi administration’s systematic throttling of free speech sets a dangerous precedent for the world.
item.dc:creator: Arman Khan

item.title: Trump Is the One Without the Cards at the Poker Table
item.description: A.I. will drastically increase the power of small states and groups in conflict with the great powers.
item.dc:creator: Thomas L. Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Evan Hume for The New York Times

item.title: Seth Meyers Gives Trump an Earful About Ear Jokes
item.description: The “Late Night” host expressed secondhand embarrassment after the president referred to the “beautiful ears” and “super hearing” of a NASA official.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Violence Erupts in Australian Town After Arrest of Suspect in Girl’s Killing
item.description: Police in Alice Springs fired rubber bullets and tear gas to disperse an angry crowd demanding justice.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Rhett Hammerton/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: A police vehicle burning outside a hospital in Alice Springs, Australia, on Thursday.

item.title: New Banksy Statue Causes Stir in Central London
item.description: The statue depicts a man marching with a flag that covers his face. It appeared in a section of London near statues of 19th-century British military and colonial figures.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Kin Cheung/Associated Press
item.media:description: The artist Banksy appeared to have taken credit for statue of a man holding a flag covering his face that stood in Waterloo Place in London.

item.title: California Police Can Start Ticketing Driverless Cars
item.description: The Department of Motor Vehicles says it could suspend or revoke permits for Waymo taxis and other driverless cars for continued violations.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
